_meta.authoringNotes: Citations cross-verified against CDC Pink Book Pertussis chapter (2021), WHO Pertussis Position Paper 2015, AAP Red Book 2024, NEJM Cherry review 2018, MMWR Tdap pregnancy effectiveness data, and 2019 IDSA practice points. Drug doses checked against AAP Red Book and CDC clinical guidance.

h1Title: Whooping Cough (Pertussis): Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Whooping cough is a highly contagious respiratory infection caused by Bordetella pertussis, a gram-negative coccobacillus whose toxins paralyse airway cilia and trigger weeks of explosive, paroxysmal coughing. The World Health Organization estimated roughly 24.1 million cases and 160,000 deaths globally each year before the COVID-19 disruption, with infants under six months bearing nearly all mortality. Disease classically runs in three stages: a mild catarrhal phase resembling a cold, a paroxysmal phase featuring violent coughing fits and the characteristic inspiratory whoop, and a long convalescent tail that earned pertussis its nickname, the 100-day cough. Untreated infections fuel outbreaks because pertussis has one of the highest basic reproduction numbers of any bacterial pathogen (R0 12-17). Macrolide antibiotics started early shorten contagiousness, and the DTaP and Tdap vaccines prevent most severe disease.

keyStats.prevalence: 24.1 million cases and 160,000 deaths globally per year (WHO 2014 modelling, pre-COVID)
keyStats.demographics: Severe disease and death concentrated in infants <6 months; adolescents and adults sustain community transmission
keyStats.avgAge: Most hospitalisations occur in babies under 3 months; resurgence cases in adolescents 11-18 years
keyStats.globalCases: Endemic worldwide; cyclical 3-5 year outbreaks even in high-vaccination countries

definition: Whooping cough (ICD-10: A37) is a toxin-mediated respiratory infection caused by the gram-negative coccobacillus Bordetella pertussis. The organism attaches to ciliated epithelium in the trachea and bronchi using filamentous haemagglutinin, pertactin, and fimbriae, then releases pertussis toxin, adenylate cyclase toxin, and tracheal cytotoxin. These exotoxins destroy ciliary clearance, paralyse local immune responses, and provoke the prolonged paroxysmal cough that defines the syndrome. The infection unfolds in three clinical stages: a catarrhal stage of one to two weeks with mild rhinorrhoea and low-grade fever, a paroxysmal stage of two to six weeks marked by clusters of forceful coughs followed by an inspiratory whoop and frequent post-tussive emesis, and a convalescent stage lasting weeks to months as cough frequency slowly fades. Infants under six months may not produce a true whoop; instead they present with apnoea, cyanosis, and feeding difficulty, which carries the highest mortality. The closely related Bordetella parapertussis causes a milder, indistinguishable clinical picture. Pertussis is managed by paediatricians, infectious disease physicians, and pulmonologists, with public health teams coordinating contact tracing, prophylaxis, and outbreak control.

typesClassification.description: Indistinguishable from a routine upper respiratory infection — rhinorrhoea, sneezing, low-grade fever, mild cough. Patients are most contagious during this stage but rarely diagnosed.

Defining phase. Sudden bursts of 5-15 forceful coughs on a single expiration, followed by a deep inspiratory whoop, post-tussive vomiting, and exhaustion. Infants under 6 months may have apnoea or bradycardia in place of whoop.

Paroxysms become less frequent and less severe but can be retriggered by viral infections, exercise, cold air, or tobacco smoke for up to 3 months. This long tail is the source of the historic nickname, the 100-day cough.
Persistent dry cough lasting more than 2 weeks, often without whoop or vomiting. Commonly misdiagnosed as viral bronchitis or asthma, yet these cases sustain household and school transmission.

Life-threatening syndrome in young infants with hyperleukocytosis (WBC often >50,000), pulmonary hypertension, refractory hypoxia, and circulatory collapse. Carries mortality above 75% once shock develops; leukoreduction or ECMO may be required.

primarySymptoms: Paroxysms of 5 to 15 forceful, staccato coughs on a single expiration, repeating in clusters dozens of times per day and often clustered overnight.

A high-pitched inspiratory whoop at the end of a paroxysm, produced as the patient gasps air past a partially closed glottis — classic but absent in many adolescents, adults, and the youngest infants.
Post-tussive vomiting that follows a coughing fit and is reported in 50-70% of school-age children with confirmed pertussis.
Apnoea, cyanosis, bradycardia, or feeding difficulty in infants under 6 months, which often replace cough as the presenting feature and carry the highest risk of death.
A mild catarrhal prodrome of 1-2 weeks with runny nose, sneezing, watery eyes, and low-grade fever — indistinguishable from a common cold, which is why most cases are missed until paroxysms begin.
Prolonged cough lasting more than 2 weeks, often described by adolescents and adults as a tickly throat that triggers uncontrollable fits at night, after exercise, or in cold air.
Subconjunctival haemorrhage, facial petechiae, or burst small blood vessels around the eyes from the pressure of repeated paroxysms.
Marked exhaustion after coughing fits, with patients describing the sensation of being unable to draw breath until the whoop arrives.
Urinary incontinence, rib pain, or rib fractures in adults from the violent force of paroxysmal coughing.
Minimal or absent fever between paroxysms — a high fever points instead to a secondary bacterial pneumonia or alternative diagnosis.
earlyWarningSigns: A child or household contact with a known pertussis case who develops mild cold symptoms — begin assessment before paroxysms appear
A cough lasting longer than 14 days that comes in fits rather than as a constant cough
Post-tussive emesis or breath-holding during cough fits in a school-age child
Any apnoea, cyanosis, or unexplained colour change in an infant under 6 months, with or without cough
emergencySigns: Apnoea, cyanosis, or bradycardia in an infant during or after a coughing spell — call emergency services immediately
Feeding refusal, lethargy, or dehydration in an infant with paroxysmal cough — admission for monitoring and intravenous support is usually required
Seizures during a cough fit or sustained altered consciousness — hypoxic encephalopathy is a recognised pertussis complication
Marked leukocytosis with white cell count above 30,000 in an unwell infant — a warning sign for malignant pertussis and pulmonary hypertension
Sudden chest pain, breathlessness, or pneumothorax-type symptoms during paroxysms in an adult — rib fracture or air leak requires imaging

causes.description: A small, fastidious gram-negative coccobacillus that lives only in humans. It binds ciliated respiratory epithelium and releases pertussis toxin, adenylate cyclase toxin, tracheal cytotoxin, and dermonecrotic toxin, which together drive the paroxysmal cough and impaired clearance characteristic of the disease.

Spread occurs through large respiratory droplets generated by coughing, sneezing, and even loud speech. The basic reproduction number is estimated at 12-17, placing pertussis among the most contagious bacterial infections — a single untreated case typically infects 80% or more of susceptible household contacts.

Acellular pertussis vaccines protect well in the short term but immunity wanes over 5-10 years. Adolescents and adults whose childhood DTaP series finished a decade earlier become susceptible again and serve as the dominant reservoir for transmission to unvaccinated infants.

Infants are most vulnerable before they complete the primary series at age 6 months. Communities with vaccination coverage below 90% experience cyclical 3-5 year outbreaks, and unvaccinated infants account for the majority of hospitalisations and deaths.

A closely related species causes a milder, clinically indistinguishable syndrome. Acellular pertussis vaccines provide little cross-protection, so parapertussis circulates alongside B. pertussis and contributes to laboratory-negative clinical cases.

Surveillance studies (Mooi 2014) document the emergence of pertactin-deficient B. pertussis strains, particularly in countries using acellular vaccines. These variants partially evade vaccine-induced immunity and may contribute to the rising case counts observed since 2010.

riskFactors.description: Maternal antibody protection is variable and the primary series is incomplete. Infants account for the majority of pertussis hospitalisations and almost all deaths; risk peaks before 3 months of age.
Children who miss doses, families who decline vaccination, and adolescents or adults overdue for Tdap boosters are 5-10 times more likely to contract pertussis than fully immunised peers.

Tdap at 27-36 weeks of pregnancy is over 78% effective at preventing pertussis hospitalisation in babies under 2 months. Pregnancies without maternal Tdap leave newborns with negligible passive protection during their highest-risk window.

riskFactors.factor: Household or close contact with an active case
riskFactors.description: Secondary attack rates within households exceed 80% among susceptible contacts. Cocooning — vaccinating everyone who lives with or cares for a newborn — substantially reduces this risk.
Nurses, paediatric clinicians, daycare workers, and teachers are exposed during the catarrhal phase before cases are recognised. CDC advises Tdap with every 10-year booster and during outbreaks.
Herd immunity for pertussis requires very high coverage because R0 is so elevated. Regions where coverage falls below the 90-94% threshold experience predictable cyclical outbreaks every 3-5 years.

HIV infection, organ transplant immunosuppression, biologic therapy, and chemotherapy increase the severity and duration of pertussis and limit vaccine responsiveness. Household contacts of immunocompromised individuals should be up to date on Tdap.

riskFactors.factor: Premature birth or chronic lung disease
riskFactors.description: Preterm infants and children with bronchopulmonary dysplasia or congenital heart disease have less respiratory reserve and decompensate quickly during paroxysms, with a higher risk of apnoea, pneumonia, and intensive care admission.

riskFactors.factor: Living in or travelling to outbreak regions
riskFactors.description: Localised outbreaks recur worldwide. WHO data show that pertussis cases doubled in several European and South American countries between 2010 and 2019, raising risk for unvaccinated travellers, especially infants.

affectedDemographics: Pre-COVID WHO estimates: 24.1 million annual cases and 160,000 deaths globally, with more than 95% of deaths occurring in infants under 1 year and most in low- and middle-income countries
United States: cyclical outbreaks of 15,000-48,000 reported cases per year between 2010 and 2019 (CDC NNDSS); under-reporting in adolescents and adults is estimated at 5-10 fold
India: pertussis remains endemic with surveillance gaps; estimated incidence of 1-2 million cases per year, concentrated in regions with sub-90% DTP3 coverage
Adolescents 11-18 years account for the largest share of reported cases in high-income countries as childhood DTaP immunity wanes
Pertussis is rare in children who have completed the DTaP primary series and 4-6 year booster; most paediatric breakthrough cases occur in those overdue for vaccination
diagnosisOverview: Clinical recognition begins with a cough lasting two weeks or longer accompanied by paroxysms, post-tussive vomiting, or whoop, or with apnoea in an infant. The CDC clinical case definition supports empirical treatment and contact tracing even before laboratory confirmation. The single most useful confirmatory test is real-time polymerase chain reaction on a nasopharyngeal swab or aspirate, which is most sensitive within the first three weeks of cough; sensitivity drops sharply after four weeks as bacterial loads fall. Culture of nasopharyngeal secretions on Regan-Lowe or Bordet-Gengou medium remains the gold-standard reference test — specificity is essentially 100% but sensitivity is 30-60% and results take 7-14 days. Serology for anti-pertussis toxin IgG becomes useful from week 2 of cough onward, particularly in adolescents and adults who present late. A complete blood count classically shows marked lymphocytosis (often 20,000-50,000) in infants and unvaccinated children, although this finding is uncommon in vaccinated adolescents. Chest radiography is reserved for suspected complications such as pneumonia, pneumothorax, or atelectasis. Differential diagnosis covers Bordetella parapertussis, adenovirus, respiratory syncytial virus, Mycoplasma pneumoniae, Chlamydia pneumoniae, foreign body aspiration, and reactive airway disease. Because public health response depends on rapid identification, infants under 1 year with any compatible illness should have PCR sent and macrolide therapy started while results are pending.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Preferred confirmatory test during the first 3 weeks of cough. Detects pertussis toxin and IS481 sequences with sensitivity of 70-95% and specificity above 99%. Can distinguish B. pertussis from B. parapertussis on multiplex panels.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A flexible swab is passed along the floor of the nose to the posterior nasopharynx and rotated for several seconds. Mildly uncomfortable; eyes may water. Results typically return within 24-48 hours.

diagnosticTests.test: Nasopharyngeal culture on Regan-Lowe or Bordet-Gengou medium
diagnosticTests.purpose: Gold-standard reference test with near-100% specificity, useful for outbreak strain typing and antibiotic susceptibility. Sensitivity is low (30-60%) and falls rapidly after 2 weeks of cough or after starting antibiotics.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Same nasopharyngeal swab procedure as PCR; the swab is plated on selective medium and incubated for 7-14 days before a result is available.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Most useful for adolescents and adults presenting after 2 weeks of cough, when PCR sensitivity falls. A single high titre or a fourfold rise on paired sera supports recent infection. Should not be used within 12 months of a Tdap booster.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard venous blood draw. Results typically take several days. Useful for retrospective public-health investigation.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Supports the diagnosis when lymphocyte counts exceed 20,000/µL — a classic finding in unvaccinated infants and young children, and a marker of severe disease in babies under 6 months.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Routine blood draw; results return same day. White cell counts above 50,000 in infants signal risk for pulmonary hypertension and intensive care admission.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies secondary bacterial pneumonia, atelectasis, pneumothorax, and pneumomediastinum. Classic appearance, when present, is perihilar infiltrate with consolidation — the so-called shaggy heart border.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Two-view radiograph completed in minutes. Read same day in most centres.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects desaturation during paroxysms and apnoea episodes in young infants. Mandatory for admitted infants under 6 months because apnoea can be the first sign of decompensation.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Continuous probe on the toe or finger. Painless; alarms are set for desaturation, bradycardia, or apnoea pauses.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of whooping cough has two aims: shorten the period of contagiousness and prevent complications, especially in infants. Macrolide antibiotics are the first-line agents recommended by the CDC, AAP Red Book, and WHO. Azithromycin is preferred because of its short course, good tolerability, and reduced association with infantile hypertrophic pyloric stenosis compared with older erythromycin formulations; the standard schedule is 10 mg/kg as a single dose on day 1, followed by 5 mg/kg once daily on days 2 through 5. Clarithromycin (7-day course) and erythromycin (14-day course) are acceptable alternatives. Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole is reserved for patients over 2 months of age who cannot tolerate macrolides. Antibiotics are most useful in the catarrhal stage and the first one to two weeks of paroxysmal cough; once paroxysms are established, antibiotics do not shorten the duration of cough but still reduce transmission. Patients become non-infectious five days into therapy, and isolation can be lifted at that point. Supportive care is the cornerstone of paediatric management: maintaining hydration, small frequent feeds, gentle suctioning, oxygen for desaturation, and admission for any infant with apnoea, cyanosis, feeding refusal, or markedly elevated white cell counts. Bronchodilators, antihistamines, and cough suppressants have not shown benefit in randomised trials and are not recommended. In severe infant disease with hyperleukocytosis and pulmonary hypertension, exchange transfusion or leukoreduction is sometimes used, and extracorporeal membrane oxygenation may be required for refractory respiratory failure. Post-exposure prophylaxis with the same macrolide regimen is offered to high-risk contacts within 21 days of exposure: infants under one year, pregnant women in the third trimester, immunocompromised individuals, and all household members of any case.

medicalTreatments.name: Azithromycin (10 mg/kg day 1, then 5 mg/kg days 2-5; maximum 500 mg day 1 and 250 mg days 2-5 in adults)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line macrolide for all ages including neonates. Five-day course, once daily dosing, well tolerated. Eliminates nasopharyngeal carriage and reduces transmission. Monitor for QT prolongation in patients on interacting medications.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Eradicates B. pertussis from the nasopharynx in over 95% of patients within 5 days; reduces secondary household transmission by roughly 70% when given within 21 days of exposure.

medicalTreatments.name: Clarithromycin (7.5 mg/kg twice daily for 7 days; adult dose 500 mg twice daily for 7 days)
medicalTreatments.description: Acceptable macrolide alternative for children over 1 month and adults. Avoid in neonates, pregnancy, and patients on strong CYP3A4 substrates. Seven-day duration may reduce adherence compared with azithromycin.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Microbiological cure in roughly 95% of treated patients in comparative trials; clinical outcomes equivalent to azithromycin.

medicalTreatments.name: Erythromycin (40-50 mg/kg/day in divided doses for 14 days; adult dose 500 mg four times daily for 14 days)
medicalTreatments.description: Original first-line agent and still used where azithromycin is unavailable. Gastrointestinal intolerance is common and the 14-day course reduces adherence. Avoid in infants under 1 month because of an association with hypertrophic pyloric stenosis.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Comparable microbiological cure to newer macrolides; clinical benefit best when started in the catarrhal stage.

medicalTreatments.name: Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (8 mg/kg/day trimethoprim component in 2 divided doses for 14 days; adult dose 160/800 mg twice daily for 14 days)
medicalTreatments.description: Reserved for patients over 2 months who cannot take macrolides. Avoid in late pregnancy, infants under 2 months, glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase deficiency, and sulfa allergy.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Microbiological efficacy similar to macrolides; less evidence for symptom benefit and higher rate of adverse drug reactions.

medicalTreatments.name: Supportive care for hospitalised infants
medicalTreatments.description: Includes humidified oxygen for desaturation, gentle nasopharyngeal suctioning before feeds, small frequent feeds or nasogastric feeding, intravenous fluids for dehydration, and continuous cardiorespiratory monitoring for apnoea and bradycardia.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces apnoea-related morbidity; admission criteria triggered by apnoea, cyanosis, feeding failure, or age under 3 months halves mortality compared with delayed admission in historical case series.

medicalTreatments.name: Leukoreduction or exchange transfusion for malignant pertussis
medicalTreatments.description: Considered for infants with white cell counts above 50,000-100,000, pulmonary hypertension, or shock. Mechanically reduces the leukocyte burden that drives microvascular obstruction in pulmonary capillaries.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Observational data and case series suggest mortality benefit when used early; randomised data are not available. Mortality in established shock remains above 75%.

medicalTreatments.name: Post-exposure macrolide prophylaxis
medicalTreatments.description: Same azithromycin regimen given to high-risk contacts within 21 days of exposure regardless of symptoms or vaccination status — infants under 1 year, third-trimester pregnant women, immunocompromised persons, all household contacts of any case, and exposed staff in maternity, paediatric, and neonatal settings.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces secondary attack rates by approximately 70% when started within 21 days of last exposure (CDC analysis).

alternativeTreatments.name: Symptomatic supportive measures (humidified air, hydration, smoke-free environment)
alternativeTreatments.description: Small Cochrane analyses (Bettiol 2014) found no benefit from cough mixtures, antihistamines, salbutamol, leukotriene receptor antagonists, or oral corticosteroids for the cough of pertussis. The only useful adjuncts are maintaining hydration, calorie intake, an upright sleep position for infants, and removing tobacco smoke and other airway irritants.

whySeeSpecialist: Refer to a paediatrician or infectious disease physician for any infant under 6 months with suspected pertussis, any patient with apnoea or cyanosis, suspected malignant pertussis with hyperleukocytosis, immunocompromised hosts, pregnant patients in the third trimester, and outbreak situations requiring contact tracing. Adolescent and adult cases with classic features can usually be managed by primary care in coordination with public health.
doctorSelectionGuide: Choose a clinician comfortable with nasopharyngeal PCR sampling, current with CDC and AAP macrolide recommendations, and familiar with reporting requirements to public health. For infants under 6 months, escalation to a paediatrician with ready access to inpatient monitoring is essential. During pregnancy, choose an obstetric provider who actively offers Tdap at 27-36 weeks, the strongest single intervention to protect newborns.
preventionStrategies: Complete the DTaP primary series at 2, 4, 6, and 15-18 months and the booster at 4-6 years — the foundation of childhood protection
Receive a Tdap booster at 11-12 years and a Td or Tdap booster every 10 years thereafter to maintain adolescent and adult immunity
Get Tdap during every pregnancy between 27 and 36 weeks gestation — over 78% effective at preventing pertussis hospitalisation in infants under 2 months (CDC vaccine effectiveness studies)
Cocoon infants by ensuring all household members, grandparents, and regular caregivers are up to date on Tdap at least 2 weeks before exposure to a newborn
Offer post-exposure macrolide prophylaxis within 21 days to high-risk contacts (infants, third-trimester pregnancies, immunocompromised, household contacts)
Maintain community DTP3 coverage above 90-94% and use targeted Tdap campaigns to interrupt cyclical 3-5 year outbreaks
lifestyleModifications: Stay home from school, work, or childcare until you have completed 5 days of macrolide therapy or 21 days from cough onset if untreated
Cover the mouth and nose during paroxysms with a tissue or disposable mask in shared spaces, and wash hands afterwards
Sleep with the head of the bed elevated and the room air humidified to reduce nocturnal paroxysms
Eat small frequent meals because large meals frequently trigger post-tussive vomiting; track and replace fluid lost through emesis
Avoid tobacco smoke, wood smoke, perfumes, and other airway irritants that can retrigger paroxysms for weeks into convalescence
Limit vigorous exercise during the paroxysmal stage; light activity is fine in convalescence as tolerated

recommended: Small frequent meals to reduce stomach distension and post-tussive vomiting
Plenty of clear fluids and oral rehydration solution to replace losses from emesis and prevent dehydration
Soft, easy-to-swallow foods (yoghurt, oatmeal, mashed vegetables) for older children with sore intercostal muscles
Foods rich in vitamin C, zinc, and protein (eggs, lean meat, citrus, leafy greens) to support recovery in convalescence
Breastmilk and frequent breastfeeds for infants — calorie-dense, hydrating, and immunologically supportive
avoid: Large meals that distend the stomach and trigger vomiting after cough paroxysms
Carbonated drinks and very cold beverages, which can provoke coughing fits in some patients
Strongly spiced or aromatic foods that irritate the airway
Over-the-counter cough syrups and codeine-based preparations, which have not shown benefit and can be harmful in children
Honey for infants under 12 months — risk of infant botulism outweighs any cough-suppressing effect

exerciseGuidelines: Rest during the paroxysmal stage; resting reduces the frequency and severity of cough episodes. As cough frequency declines in convalescence, return gradually to normal activity — walking and stretching first, then aerobic exercise as tolerated. Cold air and high-intensity exercise can retrigger paroxysms for up to 3 months, so warm up indoors and pace return to competitive sport.
dailyManagement: Complete the full 5-day azithromycin course even after cough improves, to fully eradicate carriage and reduce transmission
Use a simple cough diary to log paroxysm frequency, vomiting, sleep, and feeding — helpful for monitoring infants and for parents communicating with clinicians
Sleep upright or with the head elevated, especially in the first 2-3 weeks of paroxysmal cough
Keep the room cool, humidified, and free of tobacco smoke and strong odours
Watch infants closely during and after coughing fits for apnoea, colour change, or feeding difficulty, and seek urgent assessment if any occurs
Inform schools, employers, and close contacts of the diagnosis so that exposed individuals can be offered prophylaxis where indicated
prognosis: For adolescents and adults, the prognosis is excellent: full recovery within 6-10 weeks is the rule, although cough can linger up to 3 months. Complications such as rib fracture, urinary incontinence, and subconjunctival haemorrhage resolve once paroxysms settle. For children who have completed DTaP, breakthrough cases are typically milder and uncomplicated. The picture is very different for infants under 6 months. Hospitalisation rates exceed 50% in this group, intensive care admission approaches 20%, and case fatality is 1-2% in high-income countries and substantially higher in low-resource settings. Malignant pertussis with hyperleukocytosis, pulmonary hypertension, and cardiogenic shock carries a mortality above 75% once shock develops. Recovery from natural infection confers immunity lasting an estimated 4-20 years, while acellular vaccine immunity wanes within 5-10 years — so cases of pertussis still occur in previously vaccinated and previously infected people. Lifelong sequelae are rare; persistent reactive airway symptoms, bronchiectasis, and hypoxic encephalopathy occur in a small minority of severely affected infants.
recoveryTimeline: Catarrhal stage lasts 1-2 weeks. Paroxysmal stage spans 2-6 weeks, with attacks typically peaking around week 2-3 of the cough and slowly improving thereafter. Convalescent stage runs another 2-8 weeks, during which paroxysms become less frequent and less violent. Total illness commonly lasts 6-10 weeks; in some patients cough lingers up to 3 months, the origin of the 100-day cough nickname. Five days of azithromycin renders most patients non-infectious; isolation can be lifted at that point even if cough persists.
complications: Secondary bacterial pneumonia, which accounts for most pertussis-related deaths in older children and adults — watch for new fever, focal chest signs, or worsening dyspnoea
Apnoea, hypoxia, and seizures in infants, particularly under 3 months of age
Pulmonary hypertension and cardiogenic shock (malignant pertussis) in young infants with white cell counts above 50,000
Rib fractures, urinary incontinence, inguinal hernias, and rectal prolapse from violent paroxysms in older patients
Subconjunctival haemorrhage and facial petechiae from raised intrathoracic pressure during coughing fits
Encephalopathy from hypoxia or, rarely, direct toxin effects — occurs in roughly 0.4 per 1,000 reported cases
Otitis media, sinusitis, atelectasis, and pneumothorax as airway-pressure-related complications

supportResources.name: CDC — Pertussis (Whooping Cough) for Patients and Families
supportResources.description: Authoritative US patient education from the agency that publishes the Pink Book pertussis chapter and Tdap recommendations.

supportResources.name: WHO — Pertussis
supportResources.description: Global epidemiology, vaccination position paper, and outbreak response guidance.

supportResources.name: American Academy of Pediatrics — Red Book Pertussis Chapter (HealthyChildren)
supportResources.description: Paediatric patient guidance from the AAP, including vaccination schedules and home care for children with pertussis.

confusedWithConditions.name: Asthma
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Asthma cough is typically associated with wheeze, reversible airflow limitation on spirometry, and a response to bronchodilators. Pertussis paroxysms come in discrete violent clusters with whoop or post-tussive vomiting, lack wheeze between fits, and do not respond to inhaled bronchodilators. PCR confirms the diagnosis.

confusedWithConditions.name: Pneumonia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Bacterial pneumonia presents with persistent fever, productive cough, focal chest signs, and consolidation on chest X-ray. Pertussis runs with little or no fever between paroxysms and a normal lung exam in early disease. Pneumonia can complicate pertussis, so a febrile, breathless pertussis patient needs a chest X-ray.

confusedWithConditions.name: Chronic Sinusitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Post-nasal drip cough from chronic sinusitis is typically constant, worse on lying flat, and accompanied by facial pressure, nasal obstruction, and purulent discharge. Pertussis cough is paroxysmal, not constant, and lacks sinus-specific features. Sinus imaging and pertussis PCR distinguish them.

confusedWithConditions.name: Gastro-oesophageal Reflux Disease (GERD)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Reflux cough is typically chronic, worse after meals and at night when lying flat, and accompanied by heartburn or laryngeal symptoms. Pertussis cough comes in defined paroxysms with whoop and emesis, not a steady tickle. Empirical acid suppression does not change pertussis.

confusedWithConditions.name: Tuberculosis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: TB cough is gradual and often productive, with night sweats, weight loss, fever, and an abnormal chest X-ray (cavitation, upper-lobe infiltrate). Pertussis paroxysms are abrupt, with whoop and emesis, normal chest X-ray, and no systemic wasting. AFB sputum and pertussis PCR resolve the differential.

relatedConditions.name: Pneumonia
relatedConditions.relevance: Most common bacterial complication of pertussis and the leading cause of pertussis-related death outside infancy.

relatedConditions.name: Asthma
relatedConditions.relevance: Frequent differential and occasional co-existing condition; bronchodilator response distinguishes asthma from pertussis-driven paroxysms.

relatedConditions.name: Chronic Sinusitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Common cause of prolonged cough that mimics atypical adolescent and adult pertussis.

relatedConditions.name: Well-Child Visits
relatedConditions.relevance: Cornerstone of pertussis prevention — these visits deliver the DTaP primary series and the 4-6 year booster that protect infants and children.

relatedConditions.name: Tuberculosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Differential for chronic cough lasting more than 2 weeks, particularly in adults and in TB-endemic regions.

faqs.question: What is whooping cough?
faqs.answer: Whooping cough is a contagious respiratory infection caused by Bordetella pertussis. It produces weeks of paroxysmal coughing fits, often followed by a high-pitched inspiratory whoop and vomiting. Infants under six months are at highest risk of severe disease.

faqs.question: How long does whooping cough last?
faqs.answer: Most cases last 6 to 10 weeks across three stages: a 1-2 week catarrhal stage like a cold, a 2-6 week paroxysmal stage with violent fits, and a long convalescent tail. Cough can linger up to 3 months — the 100-day cough.

faqs.question: Is whooping cough still common?
faqs.answer: Yes. WHO estimated 24.1 million cases and 160,000 deaths globally each year pre-COVID. Cyclical outbreaks recur every 3 to 5 years even in high-vaccination countries because acellular vaccine immunity wanes within 5 to 10 years.

faqs.question: How is whooping cough spread?
faqs.answer: It spreads via respiratory droplets from coughing, sneezing, and close talk. The basic reproduction number is 12 to 17. One untreated case can infect over 80 percent of susceptible household contacts. Contagiousness ends after 5 days of macrolide therapy.

faqs.question: What does whooping cough sound like?
faqs.answer: A typical fit starts with 5 to 15 rapid forceful coughs on a single expiration. At the end the patient gasps air past a partially closed glottis, producing a high-pitched whoop, often followed by vomiting. Many adolescents, adults, and very young infants never whoop.

faqs.question: How is whooping cough diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis uses nasopharyngeal PCR, most sensitive within 3 weeks of cough. Culture is highly specific but slow. Anti-pertussis toxin IgG serology helps for late presenters past 2 weeks. Marked lymphocytosis on the CBC supports the diagnosis in infants.

faqs.question: What is the best antibiotic for whooping cough?
faqs.answer: Azithromycin is first-line for all ages including newborns: 10 mg/kg on day 1, then 5 mg/kg once daily on days 2 to 5. Clarithromycin (7 days) and erythromycin (14 days) are alternatives. TMP-SMX is reserved for patients over 2 months intolerant of macrolides.

faqs.question: Do antibiotics shorten whooping cough?
faqs.answer: Antibiotics shorten the cough only when started during the catarrhal stage or the first 1 to 2 weeks of paroxysms. After that they do not change cough duration but still reduce contagiousness within 5 days, so treatment is recommended even for late presenters.

faqs.question: How effective is the whooping cough vaccine?
faqs.answer: DTaP is 80 to 90 percent effective shortly after the primary series and wanes to 30 to 50 percent by age 10. Maternal Tdap at 27 to 36 weeks is over 78 percent effective at preventing pertussis hospitalisation in infants under 2 months.

faqs.question: When should pregnant women get the Tdap vaccine?
faqs.answer: Tdap is recommended between 27 and 36 weeks of every pregnancy, regardless of prior Tdap history. Maternal antibodies cross the placenta and protect the newborn before their first DTaP dose at 2 months. Earlier in the window means more antibody transfer.

faqs.question: Can adults get whooping cough?
faqs.answer: Yes. Acellular vaccine immunity wanes within 5 to 10 years, so adults whose childhood DTaP finished a decade earlier are susceptible. Adult pertussis often presents as a prolonged dry cough without whoop, frequently misdiagnosed as viral bronchitis or asthma.

faqs.question: Why is whooping cough dangerous for babies?
faqs.answer: Infants under 6 months have narrow airways and incomplete vaccination. They often present with apnoea, cyanosis, or feeding refusal rather than a whoop. Severe disease may progress to pulmonary hypertension and shock; case fatality is 1 to 2 percent in high-income settings.

faqs.question: Is whooping cough the same as bronchitis?
faqs.answer: No. Bronchitis is usually a viral airway inflammation with productive cough lasting 1 to 3 weeks. Pertussis is bacterial, with paroxysmal fits, whoop, post-tussive vomiting, and a 6 to 10 week course. PCR distinguishes them when cough lasts longer than 2 weeks or comes in fits.

faqs.question: Can you get whooping cough twice?
faqs.answer: Yes. Natural infection confers immunity for an estimated 4 to 20 years, and vaccine immunity wanes in 5 to 10 years. People who had pertussis or completed DTaP as children can still catch it again later in life, though repeat episodes are usually milder.

faqs.question: What is post-exposure prophylaxis for pertussis?
faqs.answer: A 5-day azithromycin course given within 21 days of exposure to high-risk contacts regardless of symptoms or vaccination status: infants under 1 year, third-trimester pregnant women, immunocompromised individuals, all household members, and maternity or paediatric staff.

faqs.question: How long are you contagious with whooping cough?
faqs.answer: Patients are contagious from the catarrhal stage until 5 days of macrolide therapy is complete. Without treatment the infectious period extends to 21 days from cough onset. After 5 days of azithromycin they can return to normal activities even if cough persists.

faqs.question: Can cough medicine help whooping cough?
faqs.answer: Trials show no benefit from cough suppressants, antihistamines, bronchodilators, leukotriene antagonists, or steroids in pertussis. The mainstay is supportive care: hydration, small frequent meals, upright sleep, and a smoke-free environment. OTC cough syrups are not recommended.

faqs.question: What is the 100-day cough?
faqs.answer: It is a traditional name for whooping cough, reflecting how long the convalescent cough can linger. Even after antibiotics, paroxysms can persist for up to 3 months and may be retriggered by viral infections, exercise, cold air, or tobacco smoke.

faqs.question: How much does whooping cough treatment cost?
faqs.answer: A generic azithromycin course typically costs USD 5 to 20 worldwide. Diagnostic PCR runs USD 30 to 150 in high-income settings and is often covered. DTaP and Tdap vaccines are free in most national immunisation programmes. Hospital care for severe infant disease is more costly.

faqs.question: Should I vaccinate my older child if a baby is coming home?
faqs.answer: Yes. Cocoon a newborn by ensuring all household members and caregivers are current on Tdap at least 2 weeks before the baby arrives. Siblings should also receive their Tdap booster at 11 to 12 years. This collective immunity is among the strongest protections for unvaccinated infants.

faqs.question: When should I take my child to the emergency department?
faqs.answer: Seek emergency care if an infant turns blue, stops breathing during or after a fit, becomes floppy or unresponsive, refuses feeds, or shows dehydration. In older patients, seek urgent care for chest pain, breathlessness, fainting during paroxysms, seizures, or blood in vomit.
